Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating COI1 could strongly alter jasmonate responsiveness for defence-related traits, but because it is a central signaling hub and required for male fertility, deployment would need tissue-specific or inducible strategies to avoid sterility and broad pleiotropy.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: COI1 is a central positive component of jasmonate signaling required for jasmonate/coronatine responses, insect and pathogen resistance, anthocyanin accumulation, root growth inhibition, and male fertility.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis of coi1; phenotyping with jasmonic acid/coronatine; pathogen and insect resistance assays; genetic interaction analysis with constitutive-response mutants.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Jasmonic acid or coronatine treatment; wounding; fungal pathogen Alternaria brassicicola; insect attack by Bradysia impatiens.
Common_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: COI1 accession not provided in review
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcript profiling/microarray-type transcript profiling of wounded opr3 leaves mentioned, accession not reported
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: COI1 encodes an F-box protein with 16 leucine-rich repeats and is inferred to recruit regulators for ubiquitin-mediated degradation. Some OPDA responses are COI1-dependent while others are COI1-independent.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of COI1 causes male sterility and increased susceptibility to insects and pathogens; strong manipulation likely causes broad defence-growth tradeoffs.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COI1 is a central component of jasmonate signaling identified from jasmonate/coronatine-insensitive mutants. 2. coi1 mutants lose defence responses to insects and fungal pathogens and are male sterile. 3. COI1-dependent and COI1-independent branches distinguish jasmonate precursor signaling, especially OPDA responses.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant defence signaling, wound response, pathogen/insect resistance, reproductive development

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CEX1 is a potential negative regulator target to elevate jasmonate-inducible defence outputs, but manipulation would likely impose constitutive defence costs and growth penalties.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of CEX1 causes constitutive jasmonate-responsive gene expression and a short-root, stunted-growth phenotype, indicating negative regulation of jasmonate signaling downstream of COI1.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant screen for constitutive jasmonate-response phenotype; expression analysis of THI2.1, VSP and PDF1.2; genetic placement downstream of COI1.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive state without exogenous treatment; comparison to jasmonic acid-response phenotype.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review notes CEX1 is more likely a jasmonate-signaling suppressor than a jasmonate-biosynthesis suppressor.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Stunted growth and short roots accompany constitutive defence activation.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. cex1 was isolated as a constitutive jasmonate-response mutant. 2. CEX1 negatively regulates jasmonate signaling downstream of COI1. 3. Loss of CEX1 activates JA-inducible defence genes but is associated with growth inhibition.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Not directly applicable; affects JA signaling outputs in seedlings
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defence gene activation and jasmonate response control

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: VSP1 promoter (reporter screen); endogenous inducible defence promoters
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CEV1 is a candidate node for co-modulating jasmonate and ethylene defence pathways, but constitutive activation causes clear developmental penalties, limiting direct breeding value unless inducible control is used.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of CEV1 causes constitutive expression of several defence genes, anthocyanin accumulation, and root developmental changes, indicating negative regulation of jasmonate and ethylene pathways upstream of COI1 and ETR1.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant screen using VSP1 promoter activation; defence gene expression assays; double mutant analysis with coi1 and etr1.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive activation background; interaction with jasmonate and ethylene signaling.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: COI1; ETR1; PDF1.2; THI2.1; VSP1; VSP2; CHI-B
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No biochemical data yet showed altered jasmonate levels in cev1 plants at the time of review.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Short roots, prolific root hairs, and constitutive anthocyanin accumulation indicate developmental and resource-allocation costs.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CEV1 negatively regulates JA/ethylene-linked defence outputs. 2. Full cev1 phenotypes require both COI1 and ETR1. 3. Constitutive defence activation causes anthocyanin accumulation and root developmental changes.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: cet loci highlight routes to constitutive jasmonate pathway activation but are unsuitable for direct deployment because of lesion mimic and lethality risks; useful mainly for dissecting defense-metabolism regulation.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Certain cet mutants constitutively elevate OPDA and jasmonic acid and activate thionin-associated defence responses, often with spontaneous lesions.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant screen for constitutive THI2.1 expression; OPDA and jasmonic acid quantification in cet1 and cet3; lesion phenotype observation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: cet1 and cet3 specifically were reported with high constitutive JA and OPDA; most cet mutants showed spontaneous leaf-cell necrosis.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Spontaneous microlesions; some dominant lines homozygous lethal.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. cet mutants identify upstream controls of constitutive jasmonate-related defence signaling. 2. cet1 and cet3 accumulate high OPDA and JA. 3. Constitutive activation is tightly associated with lesion-mimic phenotypes and lethality in some lines.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defence signaling and lesion-associated stress responses

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: DAD1 is a useful target for flower-specific manipulation of jasmonate supply and fertility traits, but editing may compromise anther dehiscence and reproductive success; tissue-specific strategies are essential.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Phospholipase A1 hydrolysis of membrane lipids at the sn1 position to release fatty acid substrates for jasmonate biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: DAD1 is essential for jasmonic acid signaling in Arabidopsis flowers; mutation causes male sterility linked to impaired precursor release for jasmonate biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis of dad1; in vitro lipase substrate-specificity assays.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: DAD1 accession not provided in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Most active in vitro against phosphatidylcholine; possible activity on OPDA-containing lipids was proposed but not tested.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Entry step from membrane lipids into jasmonate precursor release
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Mutation causes male sterility; manipulating lipid release could perturb membrane lipid homeostasis.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Free fatty acid substrates such as 18:3/16:3-derived precursors plus lysophospholipids
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Membrane lipids including phosphatidylcholine and likely galactolipids
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point in flowers; essential upstream release step
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Likely chloroplast-associated/membrane-associated context not definitively stated here
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DAD1 is required for jasmonic acid signaling in Arabidopsis flowers. 2. It hydrolyses lipids at the sn1 position to provide fatty acid substrates. 3. Mutation causes defective anther dehiscence/male sterility, linking lipid mobilization to jasmonate-mediated fertility.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S constitutive overexpression mentioned for transgenic overproduction, promoter name not explicitly stated in review
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: JMT overexpression can elevate volatile MeJA and constitutive defence gene expression, suggesting use for priming defence or volatile-mediated communication, but constitutive activation may incur growth penalties and unintended signaling effects.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group from S-adenosyl-L-methionine to jasmonic acid to form methyl jasmonate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overproduction of JMT increases MeJA levels and constitutively activates jasmonate-responsive genes without increasing jasmonic acid levels.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression; metabolite quantification of MeJA and JA; gene expression analysis.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Unwounded transgenic constitutive state; volatile signaling context.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: JMT accession not provided in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Results suggest MeJA may be perceived differently from jasmonic acid itself.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Volatile methyl-ester branch of jasmonate pathway
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely constitutive defence activation costs; specific penalties not detailed in review.
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytosol (JA can be methylated in the cytosol)
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. JMT methylates jasmonic acid to produce volatile MeJA. 2. JMT overexpression elevates MeJA but not JA. 3. Elevated MeJA is sufficient to constitutively induce JA-responsive genes, implying distinct signaling properties for MeJA.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: OPR3 is a strategic engineering point to separate OPDA- from JA-mediated traits; however, knockout causes male sterility, so partial, inducible, or tissue-specific modulation would be needed.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces the cyclopentenone ring of OPDA/dnOPDA to produce OPC intermediates for subsequent β-oxidation to jasmonic acid.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: OPR3 is required for conversion of OPDA to downstream jasmonate intermediates and for JA-dependent fertility, while its loss reveals independent signaling functions of OPDA/dnOPDA in defence gene induction.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant characterization of opr3/dde1; fertility rescue by JA but not OPDA; jasmonate metabolite profiling after wounding; transcript profiling.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wounding; fungal infection with Alternaria brassicicola; insect attack by Bradysia impatiens; greenhouse growth.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Function inferred in flowers/anthers and wounded leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Function inferred in flowers/anthers and wounded leaves
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: OPR3 accession not provided in review
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcript profiling of wounded leaves; metabolite profiling of jasmonates; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: opr3 accumulates OPDA and dnOPDA but not detectable bioactive 3R,7S-JA after wounding, enabling separation of cyclopentenone vs cyclopentanone signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Cyclopentenone-to-cyclopentanone conversion step of jasmonate biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes delayed anther dehiscence and male sterility.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for branch transition from OPDA to JA
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Peroxisome (reduction thought to take place there)
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OPR3 is the essential reductase linking OPDA/dnOPDA to jasmonic acid biosynthesis. 2. opr3 mutants are male sterile, and fertility is rescued by JA but not OPDA, proving JA requirement for male gamete development. 3. Despite no JA, opr3 retains some defence responses, revealing distinct OPDA/dnOPDA signaling roles.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Constitutive overexpression in transgenic studies mentioned; promoter not specified in review
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AOS can be used to modulate precursor entry into jasmonate biosynthesis, but overexpression alone may not reliably increase defence outputs, indicating limited standalone engineering value and need for pathway stacking.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts fatty acid hydroperoxides to unstable allene oxides en route to OPDA formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AOS overproduction did not consistently increase jasmonates or defence gene expression, indicating that simply increasing this enzyme is insufficient to drive constitutive jasmonate signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression studies in Arabidopsis and potato; jasmonate quantification; gene expression analysis.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: AOS accession not provided in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In Arabidopsis overexpression did not change jasmonates in unwounded plants; in potato it increased JA but did not alter gene expression.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No specific penalties detailed; potential futile overexpression if other pathway steps limit flux.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: 13-hydroperoxy octadecatrienoic acid and analogous hydroperoxy fatty acids
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; overexpression evidence argues it is not sufficient alone
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AOS catalyzes an early committed conversion in jasmonate biosynthesis. 2. Overexpression does not necessarily elevate constitutive jasmonate responses. 3. Pathway control therefore resides beyond simple AOS abundance.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AOC is a core chloroplast biosynthetic enzyme and a potential stacking target for tuning jasmonate production, but evidence in this review is descriptive rather than functional.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes unstable allene oxide to form OPDA/dnOPDA cyclopentenones.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AOC is a core biosynthetic enzyme producing the first cyclic jasmonate precursors OPDA/dnOPDA.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway assignment and enzyme localization summarized in review.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: AOC accession not provided in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed in pathway figure, not as a mutant focus.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AOC produces cyclic jasmonate precursors OPDA/dnOPDA in the chloroplast. 2. It functions centrally in both octadecanoid and hexadecanoid pathways. 3. The review treats AOC as a core enzymatic step defining jasmonate precursor formation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Precursor generation for defence and reproductive signaling

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Unsaturated fatty acid desaturation supplying jasmonate precursors
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: FAD3/FAD7/FAD8 collectively determine precursor supply to jasmonates; engineering them can alter defence capacity, but strong reduction severely impairs fertility and biotic resistance.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduce double bonds to generate trienoic fatty acids 18:3 and 16:3 that feed jasmonate biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: The fad3 fad7 fad8 triple mutant lacks 18:3 and 16:3, cannot synthesize normal jasmonates, and shows male sterility plus increased insect/pathogen susceptibility.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Triple mutant analysis; fatty acid deficiency phenotype; resistance and fertility assays.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fungal pathogen Pythium irregularare; insect Bradysia impatiens.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: These genes are treated genetically as precursor-supply loci rather than direct jasmonate-pathway enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Precursor lipid desaturation branch feeding octadecanoid/hexadecanoid pathways
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Male sterility and enhanced susceptibility to pathogens and insects when precursor supply is severely reduced.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Unsaturated fatty acid precursors in membrane lipids
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Membrane-associated/plastid and ER desaturase context not detailed in review
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FAD3/FAD7/FAD8 generate trienoic fatty acid precursors required for jasmonate production. 2. Triple mutants are highly jasmonate deficient. 3. Precursor deficiency compromises defence and fertility, showing the central metabolic importance of these desaturases.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: α-DOX is a candidate for engineering oxidative-stress tolerance and limiting pathogen-induced cell death, but direct metabolic products and defense consequences require further validation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Produces 2-hydroperoxy fatty acids from fatty acid substrates.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Arabidopsis α-DOX is strongly induced after Pseudomonas syringae infection and its lipid-derived products are proposed to protect tissues from oxidative stress and cell death.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathogen-induction analysis and biochemical pathway identification summarized in review.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Pseudomonas syringae infection; oxidative stress/cell death context.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Strongly induced after infection with Pseudomonas syringae
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A second enzymatic system distinct from LOX for fatty acid hydroperoxide generation in plants.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Not reported; altering cell death control may influence pathogen interactions and development.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. α-DOX defines a non-LOX route to fatty acid hydroperoxides in plants. 2. It is induced by pathogen infection in Arabidopsis. 3. Its products are proposed to protect tissues from oxidative stress and cell death.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Oxidative stress modulation and cell-death control

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CYP86A8/LCR is relevant for modifying ω-hydroxylated fatty acid-derived surface metabolites and possibly development-related lipid signals, but strong perturbation causes severe developmental defects.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates C12 to C18 fatty acids at the ω-carbon.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: LCR/CYP86A8 controls ω-hydroxylated fatty acid formation important for cuticle-associated development, with mutation causing organ fusion, altered fertility, senescence, and trichome defects.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant characterization and enzyme assignment summarized in review.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Developmental context rather than external treatment.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CYP86A8/LCR accession not provided in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Unclear whether phenotypes arise from loss of lipid signals or primarily from cuticle defects.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Organ fusions, reduced apical dominance, delayed senescence, irregular leaves, lower fertility, trichome defects.
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Endomembrane/ER-associated P450 context not specified in review
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LCR encodes CYP86A8, an ω-hydroxylase acting on medium- to long-chain fatty acids. 2. Mutation causes severe developmental abnormalities and reduced fertility. 3. The work implicates ω-hydroxylated fatty acids in cuticle biology and possibly lipid-derived developmental signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cuticle formation and possible developmental signaling

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: JAR1 manipulation could tune MeJA/JA sensitivity and defence responsiveness, but reduced signaling increases pathogen susceptibility and may weaken stress adaptation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: JAR1 is required for full methyl jasmonate sensitivity; jar1 mutants show reduced root growth inhibition by MeJA and enhanced sensitivity to Pythium irregularare.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant screen for jasmonate resistance/insensitivity; physiological assays with MeJA; disease susceptibility testing.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller of jasmonate response intensity rather than biosynthesis itself.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: MeJA treatment; pathogen challenge with Pythium irregularare.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review classifies jar1 among jasmonic acid-insensitive mutants; biochemical mechanism not discussed here.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced defence competence against at least one pathogen.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: General jasmonate-responsive growth and defence outputs
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes normal induction of JA-responsive processes; loss reduces response to MeJA
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. JAR1 is needed for normal sensitivity to MeJA. 2. jar1 mutants have reduced root-growth inhibition by MeJA. 3. Reduced JAR1 function is associated with enhanced pathogen susceptibility, linking jasmonate responsiveness to defence.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Exogenous methyl jasmonate and endogenous jasmonate cues

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: JIN1 is a possible target for modulating JA sensitivity, but review evidence is limited and insufficient for applied deployment.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: JIN1 contributes to jasmonate sensitivity, as mutants show reduced root growth inhibition by MeJA.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant screen for jasmonic acid insensitivity; root growth assay under MeJA.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Minor-to-moderate positive regulator of JA responsiveness based on mutant phenotype.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No mechanistic detail provided in this review.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. JIN1 was identified genetically as a jasmonate-insensitive mutant. 2. It is required for normal root growth inhibition by MeJA. 3. Mechanistic placement was not resolved in this review.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ETR1-mediated crosstalk could be exploited to balance ethylene and jasmonate defence traits, but because it shapes root-development phenotypes, pleiotropy is likely.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ETR1 is required for the root phenotype component of the constitutive cev1 response, indicating ethylene-pathway involvement in fatty acid-derived signal outputs.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Double mutant analysis with cev1 and pathway interpretation in review.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller of crosstalk between jasmonate and ethylene responses.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Constitutive cev1 background; ethylene signaling context.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Hormone crosstalk influencing defence and developmental outputs
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review directly places ETR1 in the JA/ethylene signaling interaction relevant to anthocyanin and root phenotypes.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Root developmental response module in cev1 background
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Ethylene receptor complex not detailed in review
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Ethylene receptor family (not detailed in review)
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Contributes to full cev1-associated defence/developmental outputs indirectly
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ETR1 participates in the full cev1 constitutive response. 2. It specifically contributes to the root phenotype branch of JA/ethylene crosstalk. 3. This highlights integration between fatty acid-derived JA signaling and ethylene signaling.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ORCA-type jasmonate-responsive TFs are attractive downstream transcriptional regulators for engineering specialized metabolism/defence, but this review only mentions them conceptually and gives no gene-specific validation here.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ORCA is noted as an important jasmonate-responsive transcription factor involved in jasmonate biological effects.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mention of prior review; no direct validation summarized here.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Acts as a jasmonate-responsive transcriptional regulator, but decisive effect is not elaborated in this article.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Gene is only mentioned in passing; detailed extraction is limited by review content.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates jasmonate-responsive genes in contexts described in referenced literature
Regulation_Genes.Source_Species: Plant species not specified in excerpt
Regulation_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Nucleus (inferred for TF, not directly stated)
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ORCA is highlighted as a jasmonate-responsive transcription factor. 2. It exemplifies downstream transcriptional execution of jasmonate signaling. 3. This review does not provide gene-level mechanistic detail beyond its importance.
